status_vocabulary.Confirmed: Reviewed and usable as a cross-spec bridge for Development Agent retrieval.
status_vocabulary.Partial: Useful bridge with at least one unresolved or approximate cross-spec association.
status_vocabulary.Seeded: Coverage placeholder present for planning and retrieval, but major associations still need curation.

rows.reconstruction_note: Subclass propagation rules are operational evidence for a SubClassOf proof step, but schema-closure rules may reflect background closure rather than a directly asserted source axiom.
EquivalentClasses often reconstructs from paired subclass-style consequences. A trace that uses cax-eqc1 or cax-eqc2 alone is usually partial evidence rather than a complete reconstructed axiom.
cax-dw gives direct operational support for pairwise disjointness; reconstruction still needs the source disjointness triple rather than just the derived contradiction pattern.

N-ary disjointness is operationalized through list-oriented support. The trace usually identifies the list structure plus pairwise consequences, so reconstructed proof objects may need an aggregation step.
DisjointUnion combines equivalence and n-ary disjointness. No single RL rule identifies the construct, so reconstruction needs to compose evidence from both families.

Intersection traces usually include both decomposition and introduction style steps. The originating list structure is needed to reconstruct the full class expression rather than only its consequences.
Union support is tied to RDF list structure plus class membership propagation. Reconstruction should preserve the original operand ordering only if the consuming proof format cares about it.
Complement reasoning is often observed indirectly through inconsistency-producing steps. The explicit complement triple is the safer reconstruction pivot.
Enumeration support depends on the RDF list that names the individuals. Reconstruct the full ObjectOneOf expression from that list, not just the derived class assertions.
Existential restriction traces may include witness-style intermediate nodes. Preserve the restriction structure and property target rather than treating the witness as part of the final proof object.
Universal restriction support often appears as propagated type assertions on property successors. Reconstruction should reattach those consequences to the originating allValuesFrom structure.
HasValue reasoning couples a property assertion with an equality check on the target. The explicit hasValue filler should remain part of the reconstructed class expression.
Unqualified max-cardinality support is identifiable, but proof reconstruction may need explicit equality-merging steps to explain why two fillers collapse.
Qualified max-cardinality combines counting pressure with a qualifier class. Reconstruction should keep both the bound and the qualifier, even if the trace only exposes equality consequences.
Subproperty traces can arise from asserted property hierarchies or schema closure. The asserted subPropertyOf triple is the right proof-object pivot when available.
Property-chain support is one of the clearer cases for trace-to-axiom reconstruction because the rule is already tied to the chain list structure.
EquivalentObjectProperties usually reconstructs from two directional property inclusions. One directional trace is evidence, not the whole axiom.
Pairwise property disjointness is directly represented, but traces may surface only through contradiction-style consequences or allDifferent support around fillers.
N-ary disjoint properties depend on list structure, so proof reconstruction should aggregate the participating properties rather than emitting only pairwise fragments.
prp-dom operationalizes the consequence of a domain axiom. Reconstruction to ObjectPropertyDomain needs the originating domain triple pattern, not just an entailed type assertion.
Range traces are usually seen as type propagation on property objects. Reconstruct the ObjectPropertyRange axiom from the source range triple, not from the propagated class assertion alone.
InverseObjectProperties is relatively direct: the trace usually exposes both directions. Reconstruction should still preserve the original property pair instead of collapsing to symmetric assertions.
Functional-property traces typically produce equality between fillers. The reconstructed axiom should refer to the property characteristic, not only the induced sameAs step.
Inverse-functional traces collapse subjects that share an object. The axiom-level proof step should remain property-centric rather than equality-centric.
Irreflexivity is usually visible only when a self-edge yields inconsistency. The source irreflexive property declaration should anchor the proof object.
Symmetry produces the reverse property assertion operationally. Reconstruction should point back to the property characteristic rather than treating the reverse edge as a standalone source fact.
Asymmetry is typically observed through contradiction when both directions occur. Preserve the property characteristic as the proof step and treat the conflicting edges as evidence.
Transitivity is one of the more direct trace-to-axiom mappings: the derived edge is operational support for the declared transitive property.
The RL operational support is shared with generic subproperty reasoning, so the row is useful but still needs validation against data-property-specific traces.
EquivalentDataProperties likely shares the generic property-equivalence operational rules, but this row should still be validated against data-property-specific examples.
The RL rule support is generic to property disjointness; treat this as a useful but still reviewable mapping for data properties.
N-ary data-property disjointness should be treated as a list-backed aggregate reconstruction, similar to the object-property case.
The operational support is shared with object-property domains. This row is usable, but we should still validate the data-property-specific reconstruction path.
Data-property ranges mix generic range propagation with datatype membership support. The row is helpful now, but the datatype side needs closer validation.
FunctionalDataProperty likely shares the generic functional-property operational rule, but this should be confirmed against datatype-valued traces.
DatatypeDefinition is not captured by a single RL rule family. The current links are intended as a starting point for datatype-oriented proof reconstruction rather than a finished mapping.
HasKey has a dedicated RL rule, which makes it a good candidate for future trace-to-proof-object reconstruction.
sameAs closure is broader than a single asserted SameIndividual axiom. Reconstruct assertions from explicit sameAs support when possible, and treat closure-only steps as derived evidence.
Pairwise DifferentIndividuals is directly supported, but traces are often easiest to interpret when paired with the conflicting sameAs evidence they block.
N-ary differentIndividuals support is list-backed. Reconstruction should aggregate the individual set rather than emit only pairwise fragments.
Negative property assertions have dedicated RL support. These are strong candidates for direct proof-object reconstruction once the corresponding StructuralElement exists.
The RL rule family is shared with negative object-property assertions. This row is a reasonable starting point, but it still needs confirmation against datatype-valued examples.
ClassAssertion is central for proof objects, but its operational support is distributed across many rule families. This row is present mainly as a retrieval anchor for future refinement.
ObjectPropertyAssertion is included as a target proof-object row, but we have not yet curated the distributed rule evidence that most often leads to it.
This assertion form is close to inverse-property support, but the proof object depends on whether the source syntax used an explicit inverse expression or an ordinary assertion.
DataPropertyAssertion is included for coverage planning. Its operational support is still distributed enough that this row should be treated as a scaffold rather than a finished mapping.
SEQ is a repository helper for RDF list handling rather than an OWL axiom. It is included here because many mapping constructs depend on ordered RDF collections.
The ontology header is structurally important for proof containers and imports management, but it does not have direct OWL RL operational support.
Ontology imports matter for closure and proof scope, but they are not represented by OWL RL entailment rules.
Version metadata belongs in the inventory because it affects ontology identity and packaging, not because it has rule-level operational support.
Datatype declarations are inventory-important even though they do not map cleanly to dedicated OWL RL operational rules.
Class declarations help inventory structural intent even when no dedicated RL rule refers to the declaration itself.
Object-property declarations are currently inventory rows rather than operational proof targets.
Data-property declarations are included for structural completeness and future feature-matrix alignment.
Annotation-property declarations are tracked for inventory completeness; they are not part of the OWL RL entailment core.
Named-individual declarations matter for structural inventory and future proof serialization, even though operational traces usually reference assertions instead.
ObjectInverseOf is a structural building block used by multiple axiom forms. It deserves its own row even though the current operational mappings are indirect.
Data-range boolean connectives are structurally important, but the direct-semantics and operational links still need deeper curation.
DataUnionOf is seeded with its semantic family, but still needs a more precise bridge into proof reconstruction workflows.
DataComplementOf is inventory-complete here, but still awaits better operational and direct-semantics linkage.
Literal enumerations belong in the structural inventory even before we have trace-driven reconstruction guidance for them.
DatatypeRestriction is likely to matter in typed-literal reasoning, but the present mapping should still be treated as an early bridge rather than a finished one.
ObjectHasSelf has a clear semantic home but no curated operational mapping yet in this seed.
Minimum cardinality is included for completeness, but its RL support and reconstruction strategy have not yet been curated.
Qualified minimum cardinality remains a scaffold row for now.
Exact cardinality generally decomposes to min and max semantics; this row exists now so the construct inventory is not silent about it.
Qualified exact cardinality is included as a structural target even though its operational reconstruction path is still open.
DataSomeValuesFrom is part of the inventory now so class-expression coverage is not missing the data-property restriction family.
The n-ary form is listed separately because the mapping uses RDF lists and deserves its own retrieval handle.
DataAllValuesFrom is present as a structural inventory row pending more precise operational linkage.
The list-backed n-ary variant is separated so Development Agents can target the precise mapping construct.
DataHasValue is a useful proof-object target even though it is not yet connected here to a curated rule-level reconstruction path.
Data minimum cardinality is included for inventory completeness.
Qualified data minimum cardinality remains a placeholder row for future refinement.
Data maximum cardinality is tracked here even though its operational consequences are not yet curated.
Qualified data maximum cardinality is retained as a scaffold row.
Exact cardinality for data restrictions is represented now so the inventory mirrors the mapping spec.
Qualified exact cardinality for data restrictions is included as an inventory placeholder.
ReflexiveObjectProperty has a clear semantic anchor but currently no curated RL operational row in this crosswalk.
Annotation assertions are intentionally present in the inventory even though they sit outside the OWL RL entailment core.
Subannotation-property axioms are tracked for structural completeness rather than current rule support.
Annotation property domain is present as a structural target pending richer semantics cross-references.
Annotation property range is included for inventory completeness.
The base annotation construct is represented now so annotation-valued structures are not absent from the master inventory.
Nested annotations are inventory rows at this stage; they will matter more once annotation-preserving proof objects exist.
Annotated-axiom support is represented by separate mapping rows because the RDF encoding shape matters for future reconstruction.
This row keeps the multiple-triple annotated-axiom encoding visible to Development Agents.
Blank-node annotated axioms are tracked separately because they affect how proof objects would need to preserve source structure.
